chunks.text: Ofcom, Riverside House, 2a Southwark Bridge Road, London SE1 9HA Switchboard: 0300 123 3000 or 020 7981 3000 www. ofcom. org. uk The Rt. Hon the Baroness Keeley Chair, Communications and Digital Select Committee House of Lords, London, SW1A 0PW DameChi Onwurah MP Chair, Science, Innovation and Technology Committee House of Commons, London, SW1A 0AA By email only Dame Melanie Dawes Chief Executive ChiefExecutive@ofcom. org. uk 8 October 2025 Dear Baroness Keeley and DameChi Onwurah MP, Online Safety Act – driving change in 2025 This letter provides an update on Ofcom’s implementation of the Online Safety Act. I hope that it is helpful to both your committees, particularly in advance of 14 th October when I will be giving evidence to the House of Lords Communications and Digital Committee. Please letusknowif you or committee members have any questions that you would like to follow up. We take our accountability to Parliament seriously, and we are a lways happy to explain our work further. Early signs of change As you know, this spring was a major turning point for the implementation of the Online Safety Act (OSA). By April Ofcom had concluded workonourtwo major Codes of Practice – to counter illegal harms online and provide wider protections for children – in line with the 18-month deadline set for us by the Act. And on 17 th March the first of the major sets of duties onplatforms – to take measures to tackle illegal harms – came in to force. Since thenwehavebeen driving compliance action across the industry including through active useofouren for cement powers. The scale of change required by the Actisvery significant, particularly when it comes to protecting children. For the last twenty years and more, since their creation, most social media, gaming, search and porn sites havebeenage-blind, operating without consideration of who their younger users are, let alone how their service needs to be tailored to the needs of children. While some companies have developed systems and processes to tackle illegal harms such as the circulation of child sexual abuse material, illegal hate and terrorist content, these protections are not applied by all, and risks online remain very high. Protections against some illegal harms such as fraud, intimate image abuse and grooming have received less attention from industry andyetare being a ccelerated by advances in technology allthetime. Above all, implementing the Online Safety Act requires culture change across the tech industry, so that safety by design is prioritised across the sector for the first time. Despite this highly challenging context, we have been encouraged by the early progress we have seen since March. Ofcom started en for cement action with in weeks of the 17 th March deadline, opening our first case on 9 thApril. Six months later we have investigations open against companies responsible for 69 services. Our early drive toenforce the rules has sent a clear message across the Page 2 of 5 industry.

I hope that it is helpful to both your committees, particularly in advance of 14 th October when I will be giving evidence to the House of Lords Communications and Digital Committee. Please letusknowif you or committee members have any questions that you would like to follow up. We take our accountability to Parliament seriously, and we are a lways happy to explain our work further. Above all, implementing the Online Safety Act requires culture change across the tech industry, so that safety by design is prioritised across the sector for the first time. Despite this highly challenging context, we have been encouraged by the early progress we have seen since March. Ofcom started en for cement action with in weeks of the 17 th March deadline, opening our first case on 9 thApril. Six months later we have investigations open against companies responsible for 69 services. Our early drive toenforce the rules has sent a clear message across the Page 2 of 5 industry.

chunks.text: for cement action with in weeks of the 17 th March deadline, opening our first case on 9 thApril. Six months later we have investigations open against companies responsible for 69 services. Our early drive toenforce the rules has sent a clear message across the Page 2 of 5 industry. All of the larger platformsare engaging with the process and have conducted risk assessments as required by the legislation. We are now scrutinising these and the measures that companies are taking to address them, through active supervision. Several small but risky sites and apps that initially failed to comply with their duties to carry out risk assessments or respond to statutory requests for in for mation have changed their approach in response to en for cement action from Ofcom, and have taken steps to begin tocomeinto compliance. Some smaller sites which are particularly risky for UK users have chosen toshutdown or geoblock the UK rather than take steps to protect UK users on their service. We believe our flexible approach to risk assessment and mitigation allows all services to take appropriate and proportionate steps to protect UK users from illegal content. But if services prefer to prevent people in the UK from accessing their sites or parts of their sites, that is their choice. Age checks The most visible and significant change sofarhasbeen the introduction of age-checks across online pornography sites, and on other sites hosting primary priority content as defined by the Act, to prevent under-18s from being able to access this content. Ofcom’s deadline for Highly Effective Age Assurance (HE A A) for user-to-user services was 25 th July and bythatdateall ofthetopten porn sites by UK users had introduced age checks, something that no other jurisdiction has achieved. At the end of September, those sites accounted formorethan a quarter of all daily visits in the UK. The majority of the top 100 most popular adult sites in the UK have now deployed an age check and our latest data suggests thatmorethan 8 million UK users are a ccessing adult sites with age checks every day. Aylo, the company which owns PornHub, the most-visited pornography site in the UK, publicly cited the considered and expert approach that Ofcom had taken, and the resolve we had already shown inenforcing against non-compliance, as key factors driving their decision to comply with the lawhere, rather than geo-block as they did in France. Xhas taken steps to implement age assurance to determine the age of its UK users, and to tailor their experiences on the site. This is particularly important because research suggests that X is the platform on which children are most likely to encounter pornography. In the first month ofthenewage-check rules being in force, visits to third-party age-check sites such as Yoti grew significantly, with 7.5 million UK visits tothetopfive age assurance providers, compared to under a million in June. We havealsoseen popular social media, dating, gaming and messaging apps introduce age assurance to prevent children accessing harmful content and improve

chunks.text: being in force, visits to third-party age-check sites such as Yoti grew significantly, with 7.5 million UK visits tothetopfive age assurance providers, compared to under a million in June. We havealsoseen popular social media, dating, gaming and messaging apps introduce age assurance to prevent children accessing harmful content and improve It is important to emphasise that while we are encouraged by these early changes, we will be checking the measures that companies have introduced, to ensure that they are working properly. Since the age check rules came in to force, there has been considerable public debate about whether and how the rules may be circumvented, including by using VPNs. VPNs are in common use in the UK and other Western democracies and offer privacy and anonymity benefits. But because they allow users to access sites and apps without revealing their real location, they offer an opportunity to circumvent the protections of the OSA. Following the 25 th July deadline we saw a spike in their use – with UK daily active users of VPN apps temporarily doubling to around 1.5 million. However, usage has since plateaued, and has now fallen back to around 1 million by the end of September. This was expected, and has happened in other countries or US states that have attempted to introduce age Page 3 of 5 checks. The key question we will be monitoring (though it is hard to measure) is whether VPN use is rising amongst children. Data from Internet Matters, collected before July, suggests that around oneinten under-18s used VPNs, with use skewing towards older teenagers. However, we are confident that the newage checks will already be offering significant protections to children – research shows that most children stumble across pornography by accident rather than seeking it out deliberately. There hasalsobeen concern from some about whether age checks are being implemented in a way that preserves privacy and protects users’ data. Ofcom has been a bsolutely clear that companies must follow UK data protection law as they design and implement age checks, and we worked closely with the In for mation Commissioner’s Office to ensure that our age assurance guidance met their requirements. As the Act rolls out, we will continue this close partnership with our fellow regulator andifwesee evidence to suggest that any company is not following data protection rules, we can refer them to the ICO. As part of our continued collaboration on age assurance, we intend to publish a further joint ICO-Ofcom statement on this topic in early 2026. This builds on our previous joint publications and will provide further clarity for sites and apps implementing age assurance on how our online safety and data protection rules fit together in practice. There havealsobeen concerns that some companies are over-moderating, preventing children and adults from seeing content that is of democratic importance or may be supportive for vulnerable groups. The OSA is clear that there is no requirement to restrict legal content for adult users – indeed in the very

chunks.text: in practice. There havealsobeen concerns that some companies are over-moderating, preventing children and adults from seeing content that is of democratic importance or may be supportive for vulnerable groups. The OSA is clear that there is no requirement to restrict legal content for adult users – indeed in the very In some cases, we are aware of specific teething problems as age checks bed in, and we are discussing these with the companies involved through our supervision teams. Compliance - supervision and en for cement As mentioned above, our supervision and en for cement teams have been working very actively to drive compliance in recent months. We are prioritising key harms with a particular focus on protection of children, including illegal content like child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and grooming; age checks and wider protections for children under the Protection of Children Code including how effectively services have configured their algorithms sothatthemost harmful material is blocked in children’s feeds; and compliance with the risk assessment duties. For larger or higher risk companies, Ofcom made a decision tosetup dedicated supervision teams in early 2024. The aim of this was to develop the detailed knowledge we needed of these services, to ensure they understand their obligations under the Act, and to give us the engagement we need to scrutinise and hold them to account for the quality of their risk assessments and the implementation of safety measures on their services. Since March we have used our statutory powers to request risk assessments for illegal harms and the protection of children from all the companies we supervise. Where we have found concerns about the quality of those risk assessments, we have already used our supervisory approach to engage with companies and drive change. In December we will publish a report on risk assessments setting outourkey findings and actions so far. We have also opened formal en for cement action where companies have not engaged and where we believe that there are significant risks to the public. We take account of all available evidence when prioritising en for cement action, including in for mation fromlawen for cement agencies (when available). We have grouped muchofthiswork in to topic-based en for cement programmes suchastherisk of CSAM on file-sharing platforms, where hash-matching technology is often not used. At this early stage of implementation of the Act, most of the companies we have targeted for Page 4 of 5 en for cement are small but risky firms who are not engaging with the newlaws, either by not responding to our in for mation requests or providing risk assessments, and/or by not implementing clear service changes suchasage checks for porn companies. However, we are also doing extensive work with the largest companies, responsible for the social media, gaming and other services on which adults and children in the UK spend most of their time. We have asked the largest social media companies – including Facebook, Instagram, Roblox, Snap, TikTok and YouTube -to report to us on the practical steps

However, we are also doing extensive work with the largest companies, responsible for the social media, gaming and other services on which adults and children in the UK spend most of their time. We have asked the largest social media companies – including Facebook, Instagram, Roblox, Snap, TikTok and YouTube -to report to us on the practical steps

chunks.text: the largest companies, responsible for the social media, gaming and other services on which adults and children in the UK spend most of their time. We have asked the largest social media companies – including Facebook, Instagram, Roblox, Snap, TikTok and YouTube -to report to us on the practical steps This includes how they are reconfiguring their algorithms to ensure that children are not pushed priority content such as violence, misogyny and dangerous challenges on their feeds, as wellasonhow primary priority content such as pornography, suicide and self-harm material is being screened out of content served to under-18s. We will not hesitate touseouren for cement powers where we have evidence of compliance breaches. Finally, we have been providing proactive support to companies, to help those who wish to comply to fulfil their duties under the OSA. Overthelast 18 months we haveheldover 400 meetings. Our regular industry bulletins help all firms in scope of the Actkeepup todatewithwhat they needtodo, and inthelastyear we haveruntwo hybrid conferences to provide opportunities for explanation, clarification and underst and ing. Measuring impact for users Our focus for this yearhas been upon tracking and underst andinghow regulated services have responded to the changes required under the OSA and we will publish a comprehensive report in December setting out our findings to date and priorities for 2026. Although we are encouraged by the early signs of change, the real proof that the OSA is making a difference will come from adults and children experiencing a difference on the services they use online. Given the scale of change that is required, this will not happen overnight. Ofcom has invested in a monitoring and evaluation programme to assess changes in user protections and measure the impact of the OSA over time. This includes tracking changes firms are making in response to regulation, gatheringdata from supervised services, analysing insights from third parties, and undertaking or commissioning bespoke research. Our research includes a multi-year, multi-faceted programme to measure children’s experiences, which includes detail on exposure to specific harms and how children are experiencing and using safety measures. We expect to publish the first setofdata and analysis evaluating the impact of changes on children’s online experiences by May. We are also setting up a children's advisory panel with the Children's Commissioner to hear directly from children about their online experiences and how they are changing. What comes next As well as driving compliance across the industry, we are also mobilising other policy and processes that still need to be implemented as part of the Online Safety Act. By the end of the 25/26 financial year, we will have: -Concluded consultation on the additional measures we have proposed to add to our Illegal Harms and Children’s Codes of Practice. -Concluded work on the handling of data preservation notices. We are currently consulting on how we intend touseourdata preservation notice powers in circumstances where coroners ask us to issue a notice, as well as the in for mation we expect to need from coroners and

We are currently consulting on how we intend touseourdata preservation notice powers in circumstances where coroners ask us to issue a notice, as well as the in for mation we expect to need from coroners and

chunks.text: and Children’s Codes of Practice. -Concluded work on the handling of data preservation notices. We are currently consulting on how we intend touseourdata preservation notice powers in circumstances where coroners ask us to issue a notice, as well as the in for mation we expect to need from coroners and Their insights are vital to ensuring that our processes are not only effective but also compassionate. We aim to publish our final statement by the end of 2025, but the powers to issue a data preservation notice came in to force on 30 th of September and if coroners seek to exercise these powers before our guidance is finalised, we will of course be ready to support them. -Finalised our Guidance on how to better protect women and girls online . This will be published by the end of November and will setout the are as where tech firms candomore to improve women and girls’ online safety. We will publish a report onplatforms’ per for mance against this Guidance 18 months later. -Concluded consultation on our recommendations on how online platformscan promote media literacy , with final recommendations published by April 2026. -Brought the super-complaints regime in to effect . Our consultation on how this will workisopen until 3 rd November 2025 with our guidance being published shortly after the relevant secondary legislation commences on 1 st January 2026. -Made further progress in implementing the fees regime . We will publish our final guidance on notification and Qualifying Worldwide Revenue (QWR) and our consultation on Ofcom’s statement of charging principles in November, and we are a iming for the final statement in January 2026 withplatforms to start paying fees in the financial year 26/27. -Technology notices. We consulted on policy proposals for minimum standards of accuracy for accredited technology that deals with CSEA and/or terrorism content, and on draft guidance to providers on how we propose to exercise this function. We expect to publish our final advice to the Secretary of State, and finalised guidance, by April 2026. Across 2026 and 2027, we will also be publishing statutory reports on age assurance, app stores and content harmful to children. We will publish an update to our implementation Roadmap, showing remaining timelines for implementation, in November, and further detail on our priorities fornextyear in our December report. Categorisation Asyoumayknow, the Government’s secondary legislation which sets the thresholds that will determine which services are categorised under the Act was subject to legal challenge, which concluded in August. While the thresholds remain in place, the Judgement raised complex human rights issues that we have been considering carefully. We will provide more detail on the timing of the register and consultation oncewehave concluded our assessment. I hope that this letter is helpful to both committees. We will be publishing a copy of it on our website so that it is a vailable to any other interested parliamentarians. Melanie Dawes

We will be publishing a copy of it on our website so that it is a vailable to any other interested parliamentarians. Melanie Dawes
